title: Vapour control layers in insulated walls and roofs
subtitle: What VCLs do, where they go, and when they are needed
excerpt: A vapour control layer prevents moisture-laden air moving through insulation and condensing on cold surfaces inside the construction. Getting the position wrong causes interstitial condensation and eventual rot. This is the decision guide.
sourceNotes: BS 5250:2021 Code of Practice for control of condensation in buildings. BRE Report BR262 on vapour control in insulated constructions. CIBSE Guide A appendix on interstitial condensation and the Glaser method.

content.text: Warm indoor air holds more moisture than cold outdoor air. When humid air diffuses through an insulated wall, it cools as it approaches the cold side. At the dew point, moisture condenses inside the construction where it cannot be seen. A
on the warm side of the insulation reduces the amount of vapour reaching the cold zone, preventing interstitial condensation.
: the insulation is vapour-open. A VCL on the warm (room) face is required to prevent moisture reaching the cold original wall surface.
Warm flat roof with mineral wool: the insulation sits below the cold waterproofing layer. A VCL on the warm side prevents moisture reaching the structural deck.
Floor insulation over a concrete slab: a 1200-gauge polythene DPM goes under the insulation boards. It acts as the vapour control layer. It blocks ground moisture from reaching the insulation.
When a VCL is not needed
PIR board used as the insulation layer:
are nearly impermeable. The board acts as the vapour control layer when all joints are taped with aluminium foil tape.
Cold roof (ventilated loft) with mineral wool on the ceiling: ventilation under the roof carries moisture away before accumulation. No VCL is needed, but the eaves must remain open.
External wall insulation (EWI): the insulation goes outside the wall, keeping the wall itself on the warm side. No vapour pressure difference drives moisture from the room into the wall.

The VCL must be on the warm side of the insulation, not the cold side. A membrane placed on the cold side traps moisture inside the insulation: vapour that diffuses in cannot escape outward. The result is worse than no membrane. The rule of thumb: at least two-thirds of the total insulation R-value should be on the cold (outside) face of the VCL. This ensures the VCL surface stays above the dew point on cold days.

Solid-wall houses built with lime mortar and lime plaster manage moisture by absorption and slow drying. Adding a VCL to the inside of a lime-plastered wall blocks moisture movement the wall was designed to handle, potentially causing damage to the fabric. For breathable construction, an intelligent (hygroscopic) VCL membrane that increases vapour resistance in winter and decreases it in summer is the correct approach. A standard polythene sheet is not appropriate.

attrs.body: The weakest point in a VCL is not the membrane but the junctions: around electrical sockets, at ceiling-wall junctions, and where the membrane meets window frames. Each gap in the VCL allows humid air through. Robust taping at all junctions with airtightness tape is as important as the membrane specification itself.
attrs.title: VCL continuity matters more than the membrane itself
